schools.summary: Typological: the Passover ('to pass over,' Exod 12:13) figures Christ's passing to the Father; the cross bound to the glory just foretold; chronology harmonised with John's six days at Bethany.
Calvin: the prediction shows how willingly Christ offered himself, since God is appeased only by a sacrifice of obedience; he forewarns lest the disciples think him dragged to death by necessity.
Fix the feast as the memorial of the Exodus (Exod 12); Robertson reckons it our Tuesday, the passover on Jewish Friday.
Henry: as his enemies prepared trouble, Christ prepared himself and his followers; fresh notices of trouble are still needed.
Explain the Passover (Exod 12:13) and the seven days of unleavened bread; Robertson dates the saying to Tuesday, the meal to Thursday; the disputed date noted (John 13:1).
Wesley details the paschal ritual — the blessed cup, unleavened bread and bitter herbs, the child's question (Exod 12:26) — as light on what follows.
Cyril: proof that Christ came to his passion willingly, foreknowing it, lest the world perish in its sins.
Leo: the Easter mystery surpasses great intellects, yet the Church may not be silent upon it.
The hidden machinery of the Passion; the guilt heightened by rank; the many 'chief priests' a sign of the state's dissolution; their caution overruled by providence.
The Sanhedrin in Caiaphas's court (high priest A.D. 18-36); the plot formed after Lazarus (John 11:49-50) and hastened by the entry and temple-cleansing.
Calvin: the priests meant to delay, so Christ's fixing of the day proves prophecy; God hastens the hour against their wish.
They dared not take him openly for fear of the people; 'took counsel' marks their puzzled state — the entry and temple debate showed his powerful following (John 11:57).
Clarke: providence frustrated their wish for secrecy, so the crucifixion was transacted openly before the festival crowds — fitting for so essential an act.
Gill: after many failed attempts they meet to form a scheme to seize him as privately as they could.
They feared a festival tumult among the vast crowds and would postpone the seizure till the feast was over, to take him by craft.
Calvin: God by a secret restraint kept them from acting at their own pleasure, and against their wish hastened the hour — Christ offered at the appointed time, not overtaken by plotting.
Wesley: human wisdom said 'not at the feast,' but when Judas came they changed, and the counsel of God set the true paschal Lamb on the great day.
Long debate over one woman or several; Simon's leprosy named to show what gave her confidence; Hilary reads her as the believing Gentiles anointing Christ's head (= God).
Identify her from John as Mary of Bethany (John 12:3); describe the alabaster flask and costly spikenard; Lightfoot equates this supper with John 12, before the Passover.
Calvin: placed here, though earlier, because Judas's indignation at it drove the priests to haste; Henry: the singular kindness of a good woman in the cleansed leper's house.

debates.question: Is the anointing woman one person or several across the Gospels?

sides.position: distinct women (Mary of Bethany not Luke's 'sinner')
possibly the same woman at different stages

outliers.note: Allegorises the woman as the believing Gentiles glorifying God in Christ's passion; the head anointed is God.

schools.summary: Identify her as Mary of Bethany (John 12:3); describe the white alabaster flask and rare ointment; Lightfoot distinguishes this head-anointing from the feet-anointing of John 12.
Gill: one woman may have done a like act at different times; this differs from the anointing in the Pharisee's house.
Judas voiced the murmur (he held the bag and was a thief, John 12:6); 'waste' = dead loss; the narratives narrow from 'the disciples' to Judas alone (John 12:4).
Calvin: the others would not have dared complain had not Judas's slander kindled them like a torch.
Wesley: several were angry and spoke, though none so warmly as Judas.
The 300 denarii nearly a year's wage (Matt 20:2); the zeal for the poor a cloak for Judas's greed (John 12:6); Mary had a right to dispose of her own, answerable to God.
Clarke: charity often serves as a cloak for covetousness — God robbed under a pretext of giving to the poor.
'Why give the woman trouble' — Jesus champions her liberty; the 'good work' is a noble, honourable deed of love upon himself, preparative to his burial (v.12).
Calvin: Christ approves not immoderate expense as a daily rule but this single act as agreeable to God for its particular reason — his burial.
Care for the poor is a standing precept (Ps 41:1; Gal 2:10), but every duty in its place — his bodily presence was only for a moment; the words searched the Traitor's heart.
Calvin: the anointing pleased God for a particular reason, not as a model — guard against contriving costly modes of worship as the Papists do.
Wesley: God's providence keeps the poor ever near, that we may always relieve them and lay up treasure in heaven.
Mary alone grasped his coming death while the disciples dreamed of a political kingdom; the verb is that of preparing a body for burial (John 19:40); Lightfoot: she first believed he should die.

Calvin: the ointment valued for its reference to his burial, a symbol that his grave would breathe life and salvation to the world (John 12:7); Wesley: not her design, but Christ's construction of it.
A proof of Christ's prescience: the universal spread of the gospel foretold at such a moment, with Mary's deed preserved by its telling as a memorial of her piety.
Calvin: estimate works by the word of God, not men's opinion; in foretelling all nations' applause Christ indirectly censures the disciples who grudged her.
Cyril turns to the eternal life the gospel offers by faith in the Son.

themes: the gospel in all the world

schools.summary: Judas acted of his own free will, afraid at the gospel's spread; Leo anatomises covetousness; the thirty pieces a dark price for the world's Redeemer.
Thirty pieces = a slave's price (Exod 21:32; Zech 11:12); Mary and Judas extreme opposites; Satan entered Judas (Luke 22:3) through his avarice.
Calvin: Christ's admonition produced no change; he went straight to his infamous bargain; Henry: the worst unkindness after the greatest kindness, by one of the Twelve.
Thirty pieces = a slave's fixed price (Exod 21:32; Zech 11:12); the priests gauged the reward in scorn; promised, not paid till after the deed (Matt 27:3; Acts 1:18).
Wesley reckons the sum at about £3 15s., the price of a slave (Exod 21:32).
Gill: 'them' are the chief priests to whom Judas brought his proposal.
The imperfect 'sought opportunity' shows Judas stuck to his business; he sought a moment 'in the absence of the multitude' (Luke 22:6) to avoid tumult.
Clarke: a fit opportunity — sin once conceived meets few obstacles till it brings forth death; a lament over the damning love of money.
The 'first day' the 14th when leaven was put away (Exod 12:18; Lev 23:5-6); the famous Synoptic/Johannine date controversy (John 18:28); the Synoptics read the Supper as the actual Passover.

The old lamb yields to the new mysteries; the day reckoned from evening; Origen: we keep the Passover spiritually, not with Jewish observances (Gal 4:4); the host's name withheld for all who receive Christ.
Calvin: the day refers to the following day; Henry: Christ, made under the law, kept its ordinances and died the day after the passover.

debates.question: Did the Last Supper coincide with the actual Paschal meal (Synoptics) or precede it (John)?

sides.position: John's reckoning leaves a different impression

schools.summary: 'Such a man' a rare Attic idiom (Mr X); the sign of the man with a water pitcher (Mark 14:13; Luke 22:10); the host a secret disciple; Peter and John sent; 'My time' = the crucifixion.
Calvin: Matthew names the man, the others give a sign, but he was doubtless an acquaintance; Christ authoritatively bids him make ready.
Wesley: 'My time is at hand' = the time of my suffering.
They procured and prepared the lamb (slain by the priests), bitter herbs, sauce, unleavened bread and wine; Peter and John sent (Luke 22:8); the passover reckoned a sacrifice.
Calvin: their ready, unquestioning obedience in seeking an unknown host, though Christ was hated, is a pattern of approved faith.
The lamb killed 'between the evenings' (Exod 12:6), eaten after sunset reclining (not standing as at first, Exod 12:11); Clarke weighs whether Christ kept it hours before the Jews.
Christ foretells the traitor without naming him, giving place to repentance; the rebuke gentle, not public; 'with the twelve' — Judas present in body though fallen in merit.
Calvin: the supper is the solemn close of the paschal rite, after which Christ came to supper.
John adds Jesus was troubled in spirit (John 13:21); 'one of you' a bolt from the blue for all but Judas; the vague form rouses the others to self-questioning.
Calvin: the baseness aggravated — an intimate friend, after his infamous bargain, present at the sacred banquet; monstrous beyond a stranger's act.

themes: one of you shall betray me

schools.summary: Each but Judas, conscious of no such intent, examines himself; 'is it I?' expects 'No' (Judas must bluff); John's by-play of glances and the sop (John 13:22); Judas feigned sorrow.
Calvin: not terror but abhorrence of the crime — each, when indirectly blamed, becomes his own judge rather than replying angrily.
The bitter-sauce dish (charoseth) into which all dipped bread by hand; Judas reclined near; the words did not yet reveal him to the rest; perhaps spoken to a few.
Calvin: Christ neither removes the doubt nor names Judas, only confirms a betrayer is present; the suspense set them to contemplate the crime and later saw Psalm 41:9 fulfilled.
'Goeth' = dies, as written (Ps 41:9/John 13:18; Isa 53; Dan 9:26); the appointment leaves men free agents and does not lessen guilt (Acts 1:16); Jesus pronounces Judas's doom.
Calvin: the betrayal takes place by God's will, proved from Scripture, yet the decree does not excuse the deed.
Wesley presses the 'woe' against universal salvation; Cyril keeps Christ's willing death in view, lest the world perish.

debates.question: How do God's decree and Judas's guilt stand together?

sides.position: the cross was written/determined, yet Judas remains a free, guilty agent

schools.summary: 'Thou hast said' = yes, thou art the man (cf. Matt 26:64); Judas alone says 'Rabbi,' not 'Lord'; after the sop Satan entered him (John 13:27); the answer not heard by all.
Calvin: hardened guilt mixes boldness with torment — Judas, restrained by an evil conscience, cannot be silent yet is overwhelmed with fear.
Sacramental realism: the true paschal sacrament, foreshadowed by Melchizedek (Gen 14:18); the Body delivered under the form of bread; Judas's sin aggravated at the mystery.
Cyril: the faithful are made 'of the same body and blood with Christ'; Chrysostom on Judas approaching unchanged.
Calvin: two symbols showing our life is shut up in Christ, who supplies the place of meat and drink.
Wesley/Clarke: 'this is' = signifies/represents, in the style of Scripture (Gen 40:12; Gal 4:24).
Barnes: 'this represents my body'; Robertson: the body not 'broken' (John 19:33; genuine text of 1 Cor 11:24).
Henry: instituted at the close of the passover, in the room of it — Christ our Passover sacrificed for us (1 Cor 5:7).

debates.question: In what sense is the bread Christ's body?

sides.position: a real participation in the Body (sacramental realism)
a sign that 'signifies/represents' the body, sealing union by faith

schools.summary: The cup of his blood, 'the blood of the covenant' (Exod 24:8), 'for many'; bread and wine separated to represent the Passion; the mingled water from his pierced side; thanksgiving shows a willing passion.
The cup of the Hallel, taken after supper (1 Cor 11:25; cf. v.30); the later ritual's three cups (Luke 22:17).
Calvin: two symbols to fix faith on Christ, who supplies the place of meat and drink — a display of his grace.
'Testament' = covenant (diatheke; Heb 9:15-17; Gen 15:9 'cut' a covenant); best MSS omit 'new' here; the error of reading 'New Testament' as the book corrected.
Gill: the cup an emblem of his blood, ratifying the covenant of grace and conveying its blessings (Exod 24:8).
Wesley reads 'for many' wide — as many as spring from Adam.
Cyril finds the mystical table of Psalm 23:5 set in place of the table of devils.
The passover rites here end; the last cup before death, with a forward look to the 'new' wine of the perfected kingdom; 'fruit of the vine' not to be pressed literally for the Messianic banquet.
Calvin: the disciples warned of his departure and the hope beyond; the precise moment of the saying matters little.
Wesley: no more wine till he drinks it of another kind in the Father's kingdom, which they too shall share.
The hymn is the Hallel (Ps 113-118), sung in two parts; they tarried for the discourse and prayer of John 14-17 before going out (John 14:31; Exod 12:22).
Moralise the closing hymn and the ascent of Olivet — praise that rises 'from height to height'; the hymn linked to John 17 and Ps 22:26.
'Offended' = stumbled, forsake and lose confidence; the citation of Zech 13:7 shows Christ's mind dwelt there (cf. John 16:32); occasioned by Peter's boast (John 13:37).
Calvin: spoken to all but chiefly in Peter's person, who needed extra consolation lest, after denying, he despair.
Wesley: something will happen that occasions their falling by forsaking him (Zech 13:7).
Henry: a scattering storm — fright would rob them of courage to cleave to him.
A word of comfort and a tryst — he will meet them in Galilee (Mark 16:7; Matt 28:16); the shepherd revives and gathers the flock; yet the words fell unheeded at the time.
Gill/Calvin: the risen Shepherd gathers the scattered sheep; God still owns those driven out for a time — comfort against despair.
Wesley: though you forsake me, I will not forsake you.
Peter's boast sprang from true love but too much self-reliance and self-ignorance; he ignored the resurrection word, intent on surpassing 'all' the rest; harmonists note the dislocated order.
Calvin: sincere affection, but false confidence carries him into foolish boasting — justly reproved for not examining himself.
Clarke: the presumptuous man promises everything and performs nothing; we know ourselves least of all.
Mark/Luke add 'before the cock crow twice'; the morning cock-crowing (Mark 13:35); 'within the time of cock-crowing' (John 13:38); a denial not gradual but this very night — the prevision superhuman.
Wesley dates it before three a.m., the usual cock-crowing, though one cock crowed after the first denial.
Cyril, on Christ's foreknowledge, recalls the like 'Verily' to the penitent thief (Luke 23:43).

Peter spoke the more vehemently with a strong negation; all joined the profession yet all fled (Matt 26:56); a sincere attachment that must fail without God's power; all dared to die (cf. John 11:16; Rom 5:7).
Wesley: such was the Lord's tenderness that he would not aggravate their sin by reply.
Gethsemane ('oil-press') an olive orchard beyond Kidron at Olivet's foot (John 18:1); Calvin: Christ came as he was wont, presenting himself to death (John 18:2) — his obedience.
Defend the reality of his sorrow against heretics: the impassible Deity remained while the assumed human soul truly suffered (Ambrose, Jerome, Augustine); he prayed apart to teach us retirement.
Henry: the beginning of sorrows — the sword awakes against the man that was God's Fellow.

debates.question: Does Christ's fear and sorrow imply he was a mere creature?

sides.position: No — the impassible Deity remained; the true human soul he assumed truly suffered

schools.summary: The three 'chosen out of the chosen' as at Jairus's house (Luke 8:51) and the Transfiguration (Matt 17:1), taken for human sympathy; 'very heavy' a far stronger word — sorrow dissolving natural vigour.
Wesley: the anguish from the arrows of the Almighty as God laid on him the iniquities of us all — an exquisite, penetrating sorrow.
Calvin: the true test of virtue as Christ fights temptation face to face; the great contest after preparatory exercises.
His human soul deeply affected; 'even unto death' = extreme, indescribable anguish ('ready to die'); 'watch' = keep awake; he craved a little human sympathy.
Calvin: he shares his sorrow to rouse them to sympathy and shame their carelessness; the phrase a deadly wound of grief (cf. Jonah 4:9); rebukes the ancients' fear of admitting his true grief.
The prayer proves Christ's true human nature, willing apart yet yielding (Augustine); Hilary reads 'let it pass to my disciples'; Leo: the health of the whole body.
Calvin: a genuine, abrupt prayer of real horror at God's wrath (the bloody sweat); the two wills harmonize without conflict, against the Monothelites.
The atonement's necessity (Hebrews 5:8); his human will shrinks but surrenders — 'here Jesus won the power to go on to Calvary' (Robertson).

outliers.note: Reads 'let this cup pass' as a prayer that the disciples might drink it without fear, not that Christ might avoid it.

schools.summary: The disciples slept 'for sorrow' (a known effect of grief), after 24 hours awake — not indifference; the reproof falls especially on the boastful Peter.
Calvin: a lesson on prayer's rhythm (cease and return); the friends' indifference was a bitter aggravation of Christ's voluntarily-borne grief.

outliers.note: The JFB fragment here belongs to the woes of chapter 23, not this verse.

themes: could ye not watch one hour

schools.summary: 'Watch' became the early Church's watchword; a tender warning recognizing the willing spirit while the flesh wars against it (Galatians 5:17); cf. the Lord's Prayer (Matthew 6:13).
Calvin: resist not by self-confidence but by a conviction of weakness that drives us to ask strength from God; perseverance in prayer is promised victory.
Clarke/Wesley: a gentle rebuke and kind apology — watch lest taken unawares, pray to be enabled to bear the trial.
A deepened acceptance — the cup cannot pass, so 'thy will be done'; Barnes defends the bloody sweat and the strengthening angel (Luke 22:43-44), Christ strengthened as a man (Hebrews 1:14).
Calvin: he ceases to ask removal and insists on obeying the Father; the first struggle animated him to greater confidence.
Clarke: his felt suffering and perfect resignation prove the atonement was real and a free-will offering.
The drowsiness echoes the Transfiguration (Luke 9:32); Christ returns craving sympathy, finds them stupefied (Mark 14:40); Clarke wonders if Satan weighed their eyes.
Calvin: the sleep came from immoderate sorrow — Satan exploits both security and grief; even the chosen three fall asleep again after reproof.
The threefold prayer (cf. Paul, 2 Corinthians 12:8) marks deep earnestness; repetition of 'the same words' is intensity, not formalism (Ellicott), distinct from heathen vain repetition (Matthew 6:7).
Augustine: a pause for rest then rousing (Mark's 'it is enough'); Hilary allegorizes the three findings as the Church's history; Jerome: Christ offers himself willingly.
A question or mournful irony; the sudden change marks Judas's approach — 'the Master's time of weakness is past' (Robertson).
Calvin: ironic — the enemies will now compel them to watch against their will; the chastisement of those deaf to words.

debates.question: Is 'Sleep on now' permission, irony, or a question?

sides.position: Real permission followed by a pause, then rousing

schools.summary: Not flight but a call to confront the betrayer; Christ goes willingly to die for his people; 'is at hand' echoes the Baptist (Matthew 3:2).
Calvin: the seal of Gethsemane's victory — new strength from heaven (Hebrews 5:7); the trembling subdued, he cheerfully advances to death.

outliers.note: The JFB fragment here belongs to chapter 5 on oaths, not this verse.

schools.summary: The band = the Roman cohort (swords) plus temple officers (clubs), with torches (John 18:3); 'one of the twelve' emphasizes the horror of Judas's betrayal.
Henry: Christ stood abandoned as a sacrifice, made a curse for us; none are more abhorred than disciples who betray with a kiss.
Calvin: wicked men execute God's secret purpose; the excessive force betrays their tormented conscience before Christ's divine power.
Leo: the crucifixion the most wondrous mercy, the two Natures meeting in the Redeemer; Origen: Christ is still betrayed when his own keep sinning.
The sign was needed (night, similar dress, soldiers who had not seen him); Mark's pluperfect shows it was prearranged; the kiss a perverted token of friendship.
Calvin: Judas, restrained by shame, hides behind hypocrisy; 'hold him fast' betrays his memory of Christ's divine power — astonishing madness to oppose it.
Clarke: cold, deliberate, hypocritical malice — Judas outdoes even Joab's treacherous greeting of Amasa.
'Hail, Rabbi' and the kiss are the warmest tokens of friendship perverted; Lightfoot notes a disciple kissing his master was unusual — respect or derision.
Calvin: feigned compassion ('Master, Master') and a farewell kiss; the devil so fascinated Judas that he thought his treachery concealed.
'Friend' is the cooler word ('comrade,' as Matthew 20:13; 22:12); 'wherefore art thou come' likely a sharp question ('do your errand,' Deissmann) to prick his conscience.
Calvin: a grave charge of ingratitude (Psalms 41:9), warning that the Church will always cherish traitors — worship God with sincerity.
Wesley: Christ meets the malignant kiss with composure and dignity, leading captivity captive.
The swordsman is Peter (named only by John for his protection), the servant Malchus; the two swords (Luke 22:38) were for warning, not war; Christ's healing a proof of deity (Clarke).
Allegory of the cut ear (the disobedient hearing of the priest-enslaved people); Augustine: Peter sinned through warm but unauthorized zeal, like Moses.
Calvin: rash zeal condemned — we are readier to fight than bear the cross; no finger should move except as God commands.
Chiefly a caution to Peter (a proverb); not a rule against all warfare but a warning that Christ's cause is not defended by the sword (2 Corinthians 10:4); offensive war condemned.
Calvin: forbids private violence (court of conscience) while upholding the magistrate's God-given sword; lawful self-defense needs a heart free of revenge.
Augustine: unlawful is the sword taken without legitimate authority; Leo: it was contrary to the redemption that Christ refuse to be taken.

debates.question: How far does 'all that take the sword shall perish' extend?

sides.position: A caution to Peter / against defending Christ's cause by the sword
A prophecy of the Jewish and Roman states

outliers.note: The JFB fragment here belongs to chapter 23, not this verse.

schools.summary: Twelve legions (perhaps one per apostle) show Christ suffered voluntarily, with infinite power at hand (cf. Elisha, 2 Kings 6:17); 'presently' = immediately.
Calvin: he speaks conditionally — he could ask, but refrains because rescue is contrary to the Father's decree; resorting to unlawful means dishonors God.
The necessity foretold (Psalms 22; 69; Isaiah 53; Daniel 9) is the ground of his submission and the source of his calm (Hebrews 2:10); his death no accident.
Calvin: Christ obeys the Father's revealed will; Scripture is a bridle subduing the flesh's rebellion (Romans 15:4).

themes: how shall the scriptures be fulfilled

schools.summary: 'Robber' (as Barabbas, John 18:40) not 'thief'; the daily temple teaching points to a wider Jerusalem ministry; Christ hunted as the worst of men in deep humiliation.
Chrysostom: they came by night fearing the crowd; Christ went forth to give them opportunity — proof he suffered of his own free choice.
Calvin: the armed display exposes their malice and trembling conscience before an unarmed teacher.
Christ's own words: all fulfils the prophets (Isaiah 53; Zechariah 13:7); the disciples' flight excused in part by exhaustion and the command not to resist; Mark's naked young man.
Calvin: God's hand prevails over the rage of Satan and men, yet they remain inexcusable; the flight removes the offense of the cross for weak minds.
First to Annas, then Caiaphas (already committed to condemnation, John 11:49); the night session was illegal — a packed, informal meeting until daybreak.
Henry: following 'afar off' is beginning to go back; yet Christ now openly confesses he is the Christ, an example to confess him before men.
Calvin: the visible Church of God conspires to extinguish the only hope of salvation — the rejected cornerstone (Psalms 118:22); foretold, lest believers be perplexed.
Following 'afar off' mingles love and fear — the first step of the fall; admitted by John, he sits at the enemy's fire 'to see the end' with restless anxiety, not devotion (Ellicott).
Clarke/Wesley: the weak in faith should shun dangerous places and company; unfit companions proved Peter's snare.
A foregone verdict seeking false witness (a continued search); the two-witness rule (Deuteronomy 17:6) defeated by contradictions; judges turned prosecutors (Robertson).
Chrysostom: a den of robbers masquerading as a trial; Origen: their failure proves Christ's irreproachable life.
Calvin: no equity where the cause is not first examined; Christ's innocence shines so brightly that even devils might know he died innocent.
Many false witnesses came but disagreed (Mark 14:56); the two-witness law could not be met, for falsehood is self-contradictory; God guarded Christ from a true charge (Clarke).
Calvin: 'false witnesses' here pervert what was justly said (the temple-saying); the triviality shows the priests blinded by fury.
A distortion of John 2:19 (the temple of his body) into a threat against the stone temple; even so the witnesses disagreed (Mark 14:59) — holy words twisted into a capital charge.
The high priest, vexed by failure and Christ's silence, tries to extract self-incrimination; the silence fulfils Isaiah 53:7 (cf. 1 Peter 2:23).
Calvin: Caiaphas wickedly insinuates guilt from the accusations; Christ, appointed to be a sacrifice, lays aside all anxiety to defend himself.
The adjuration (an oath of compurgation) is craftily framed to force denial or 'blasphemy'; Christ answers truth under oath though silent before lies (cf. Matthew 5:34).
Calvin: Caiaphas swears in bad faith, already resolved on death; the title 'Son of God' for the Messiah was known from Scripture.
'Thou hast said' = 'I am' (Mark); Christ claims to be the Daniel 7:13 Son of Man and future Judge; Ellicott: decisive against any merely-human view of Christ.
Calvin: he confesses to inflame their rage, not avoid death; the despised prisoner will come as Judge — terror to the wicked, hope to believers.
Wesley: he looked unlike that person now, yet nothing more awful and majestic than this admonition.

outliers.note: The JFB fragment here belongs to chapter 23, not this verse.

themes: Son of man on the right hand

schools.summary: Rending the garment was the prescribed formal sign of condemnation for blasphemy (Lightfoot); they never examined whether his Messianic claim was true, assuming him an impostor.
Calvin: the rending is a hypocrite's false zeal — yet it teaches how seriously real blasphemy should be regarded, against cowardly indifference.

A unanimous verdict 'guilty of death' (the older idiom for deserved punishment); blasphemy's penalty was stoning (Leviticus 24:16), but they plan to deliver him to Rome for crucifixion (John 18:31); Joseph and Nicodemus absent.
Clarke: all judicial order violated — the judge becomes accuser, condemning without examining the prophecies and miracles.
Spitting and buffeting (the parsed Greek) signify highest contempt, fulfilling Isaiah 50:6 and 52:14; Christ bears all with meekness, without a word of reply.
Chrysostom: their intoxicated madness; the evangelists honestly record the shame, counting it glory that the Lord of all suffered thus for us.
A mockery of Christ's prophetic office, sharpened by the blindfold (Mark/Luke); the taunt 'Prophesy' unwittingly proclaims the Prophet whose words were then being fulfilled (Luke 18:32; v.34).
Henry: they made sport with his prophetic office, like Belshazzar's revels — profanely jesting with holy things.
Ellicott harmonizes the three denials; the impulse was shame as much as fear (cf. Matthew 16:22); Peter sat in the firelight where he was recognized.
Henry: bad company ensnares; to dissemble knowledge of Christ is in effect to deny him; Peter fell by surprise, not by design like Judas.
Calvin: a mirror of our weakness for the whole Church; rash to enter the hall, Peter falls at a woman's voice — any man unupheld falls at a falling leaf.
A public denial (aggravation); Peter denies being a disciple, feigning ignorance — a transparent dodge; the second gradation of his fall.
Calvin: denying before many harms the weak; though he denies only acquaintance, he indirectly buries the light of redemption — evasive ambiguity gains nothing.
Peter edges to the porch to escape notice but is exposed afresh; the accusation repeated by maid and man (harmonizing the Gospels); the first cock-crow sounds (Mark).
Calvin: the maid's word flew from lip to lip, drawing in a multitude (John); Christ's 'cock-crow' covers the whole period of crowing, reconciling the accounts.
The third gradation — denial sealed with an oath, forgetting Christ's command (Matthew 5:34); Peter calls Jesus 'the man' with possible contempt.
Calvin: sin hurries from bad to worse once the Spirit's help is withdrawn; yet God mercifully restrains the enemies' tongues lest Peter be overwhelmed.
The third accusation, an hour later (Luke 22:59), prompted by Peter's Galilean accent (the gutturals, Sh/Th); John adds the kinsman of Malchus saw him in the garden.
Wesley: God's providence let the world catch hold of the weakest argument; the half-detection drove Peter to a more vehement denial.
The final gradation — cursing (imprecations on himself) and swearing; 'a cock crew' (the dawn hour), the appointed sign Christ foretold (v.34).
Calvin: his unfaithfulness reaches its height, abandoning body and soul — yet this is weakness, not the sin against the Spirit (Matthew 12:31).

The remembered word and the Lord's look (Luke 22:61) open the flood-gates of penitence; Barnes' eight lessons — self-confidence's danger, deep repentance, no presuming to sin; the evangelists' honest record (cf. 1 Timothy 1:12-16).
Henry: the cock-crow a John Baptist calling to repentance; secret, serious, bitter weeping — Peter never denied him again but confessed him openly.
